---
draft: false
title: "Climate-health hot spots in Brazilian agriculture"
description: "Occupational health surveillance data (2003--2024) for agricultural workers in Brazil, covering work-related accidents, climate-linked hospital admissions, dengue, agrochemical poisoning, and violence. Supports the identification of climate-health risk hot spots at the municipality level."
date: 2026-02-10
date-modified: 2026-03-01
categories: [Dataset, Climate Hazard, Occupational Health, Brazil, CLIDEWO]
image: ../../assets/images/img-timeseries.svg
---

[← Back to Portfolio](../../portfolio.html){.back-link}

::: {.label}
Dataset · Brazil · 2003--2024 · Zenodo · CC BY-NC 4.0
:::

Climate change affects agricultural workers first through their health. This dataset assembles two decades of occupational health surveillance records to make that connection visible at the municipality level across Brazil.

Two national systems contribute the data. SINAN (the notifiable diseases reporting system) provides incidence records from 2009 to 2024, covering work-related accidents, dengue cases, agrochemical poisoning, and violence among agricultural workers. SIHSUS (the national hospital admissions system) provides inpatient records from 2003 to 2024, filtered to approximately 900 ICD-10 diagnoses linked to six climate-driven occupational health pathways: excessive heat, air pollution, UV radiation, vector-borne diseases, agrochemical exposure, and waterborne diseases.

The dataset is the empirical foundation for the paper "Mapping Climate-Health Hot Spots: Implications for Workers' Health and Decent Work in Brazilian Agriculture."

## Coverage

| Source | Years | Records type |
|--------|-------|-------------|
| SINAN | 2009--2024 | Notifiable disease incidence |
| SIHSUS | 2003--2024 | Hospital admissions (~900 ICD-10 codes) |

Both sources are disaggregated to municipality level using IBGE codes, so they can be combined with other municipal-level climate and socio-economic data.

## File size

164.6 MB (restricted access).

## Access

Access is restricted. Requests can be submitted through the Zenodo record with a brief description of intended use. The restriction is in place during peer review and will be lifted on publication.

DOI: [10.5281/zenodo.18632647](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18632647)

## License

CC BY-NC 4.0 — non-commercial use with attribution.

## Funding

British Academy ODA Challenge-Oriented Research Grants 2024 Programme (IOCRG100945), via the UK Government's International Science Partnerships Fund.
